hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: Congregation gathered for a church service in a rural Black church, Heard County, Georgia, 1941
inline_images.caption: A church service in Heard County, Georgia, photographed by Jack Delano in 1941, the rural Black congregation as the communal center that Of the Faith of the Fathers places at the heart of African American life.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information Collection)
inline_images.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: No known restrictions; FSA/OWI photograph)

related_chapters.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
related_chapters.topic: The Black Church and the Movement
related_chapters.why: The essay casts the Black church as the governing social center of Negro life; Walker describes how movement rallies were themselves church services.

related_chapters.subject: Worth W. Long
related_chapters.part_title: The Color Line and a Local Hero
related_chapters.topic: McKissick, the NAACP, and the Church as Center
related_chapters.why: The essay calls the church the central club-house and authority of the community; Long recounts the church as organizing center with an NAACP chapter in every congregation.

related_chapters.subject: Samuel Berry McKinney
related_chapters.chapter_title: Religion Functions on the Front End
related_chapters.why: The essay portrays the Preacher and the church's ethical leadership of the people; McKinney, a pastor, reflects on the social gospel and religion functioning at the front of public life.

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Church and the School as Centers
related_chapters.why: The essay names the church as the social hub binding a proscribed people together; Terry recalls the church and the school as the centers of a connected Black community.

related_chapters.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
related_chapters.chapter_title: Baptist, Methodist, and the Sanctified Church
related_chapters.why: The essay traces how Negro religion became overwhelmingly Baptist and Methodist; Burns describes a family of faith spanning the Baptist, Methodist, and Sanctified churches.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
related_chapters.why: The essay shows the church as the social, intellectual, and economic centre of community life; Derby recalls the church as the hub of her semi-rural Bronx neighborhood.

top_voice.subject: Wyatt Tee Walker
top_voice.reason: His description of rallies as church services captures the essay's thesis that the Black church is the community's social government and the seat of its collective striving.

closing_image.caption: Gillfield Baptist Church, Petersburg, Virginia. Wyatt Tee Walker is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of the Faith of the Fathers raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman, Abernathy family, and William Lamar Strickland.

nearest_voices.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
